criteria: The agent has read Section 3 to understand the overall GMAIL framework: two separate encoders (f_r for real images kept frozen, f_g for generated images fine-tuned with LoRA), the Gen-CLIP flow for training, and the CLIP flow for inference on real images.

The agent has read Section 3.2 and Eq. 1 to understand the cross-modality alignment loss that minimizes the distance between generated image embeddings f_g(x_g) and real image embeddings f_r(x_r) paired with the same caption via contrastive learning with temperature τ.

The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand how the aligned Gen-CLIP model integrates with downstream vision-language models (CLIPCap, LLaVA, Llama3): the aligned f_g is used to process generated images for VLM fine-tuning, while f_r is used at inference on real images.

The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the implementation details: CLIP base model, LoRA rank 4, temperature τ=0.07, AdamW optimizer with lr=1e-4 and weight decay=0.01, cosine annealing with warm restarts, batch size 256, and 50,000 fine-tuning steps for projections.

The agent has read Appendix A to understand the dataset-specific image generation details: 560k images for COCO, 3.3M for CC3M, and 12M for CC12M, all generated using Stable Diffusion v2 with 50 inference steps.
The plan includes setting up a dual-encoder architecture with a frozen pretrained CLIP model f_r for real images and a separate CLIP model f_g for generated images that is fine-tuned using LoRA.
The plan includes implementing the cross-modality alignment loss (Eq. 1) that computes contrastive loss between generated and real image embeddings using cosine similarity and temperature scaling.
The plan includes generating synthetic training images using Stable Diffusion v2 conditioned on captions from COCO train2014, and optionally CC3M and CC12M, with 50 inference steps per image.
The plan includes separate projection heads for real images ('Proj for Real') and generated images ('Proj for Gen'), fine-tuned for 50,000 steps.
The plan includes integrating the aligned CLIP encoder with downstream VLMs (at least CLIPCap and LLaVA) for fine-tuning on image captioning with generated data.
The plan includes running image captioning experiments on COCO with ClipCap, LLaVA, and Llama3, with and without GMAIL, and reporting B@4, METEOR, CIDEr, SPICE, ROUGE-L, and WMD metrics.

The plan includes zero-shot image retrieval evaluation on COCO and Flickr30k benchmarks with CLIP and Long-CLIP as base models, reporting R@1, R@5, and R@10 for both image-to-text and text-to-image directions.
The plan includes zero-shot image classification evaluation across eight datasets (DTD, Stanford Cars, SUN397, Food 101, Aircraft, Oxford Pets, Caltech 101, ImageNet 1K) using top-1 accuracy.
The plan includes ablation studies comparing models with and without Gen-Real alignment, and ablation over LoRA ranks (2, 4, 6) and full fine-tuning on image captioning.
The dual CLIP encoder architecture is implemented: f_r is the frozen pretrained CLIP image encoder and f_g is a separate copy of the same CLIP image encoder that will be fine-tuned with LoRA.
LoRA is integrated into the image encoder f_g with rank=4, enabling low-rank adaptation of the CLIP image encoder for generated images while preserving pretrained weights.

The cross-modality alignment loss (Eq. 1) is correctly implemented as a contrastive loss: for each batch, it computes cosine similarity between f_g(x_g) and all f_r(x_r') in the batch, applies temperature scaling with τ=0.07, and uses the log-softmax of the similarity of matched pairs.
Separate projection heads ('Proj for Real' and 'Proj for Gen') are implemented and fine-tuned, connecting respectively the real and generated image encoders to downstream VLMs such as CLIPCap.

The training loop for Gen-CLIP flow is implemented: batches of (x_g, x_r) image pairs sharing the same caption are processed, alignment loss is computed, and LoRA parameters of f_g are updated via AdamW (lr=1e-4, weight decay=0.01) with cosine annealing schedule with warm restarts.
Batch normalization and gradient clipping are applied during training to stabilize the Gen-CLIP fine-tuning.
The training data loader supports a batch size of 256 and yields (x_g, x_r) pairs where x_g and x_r share the same text caption.
Synthetic image generation pipeline is implemented using Stable Diffusion v2, taking COCO train2014 captions as input and generating corresponding synthetic images with 50 denoising steps.

The inference pipeline for real images uses the original frozen CLIP encoder f_r (with 'Proj for Real') rather than the generated-image-fine-tuned f_g, correctly separating the two flows at inference time.

GMAIL is integrated with ClipCap for image captioning: the aligned generated image embeddings from f_g are used to fine-tune ClipCap's transformer prefix mapping, while at inference real image embeddings from f_r are used.
GMAIL is integrated with LLaVA for image captioning and VQA: the aligned CLIP encoder is used as the vision backbone, fine-tuned on generated images, and used with the real encoder at inference.

Zero-shot image retrieval evaluation is implemented: image and text embeddings are computed, cosine similarities are ranked, and Recall@1, Recall@5, Recall@10 are reported for both image-to-text and text-to-image directions.
Zero-shot image classification evaluation follows the original CLIP setup: natural language prompts guide classification across unseen categories and top-1 accuracy is reported.
Image captioning evaluation computes all six metrics: BLEU@4, METEOR, CIDEr, SPICE, ROUGE-L, and WMD.
The scaling trend experiment is implemented: GMAIL is trained on generated images derived from COCO (560k), CC3M (3.3M), and CC12M (12M) and evaluated on zero-shot retrieval on Flickr30k.
The LoRA rank ablation is implemented by running training with LoRA ranks 2, 4, and 6, plus a full fine-tuning baseline, and evaluating image captioning performance.
The Gen-CLIP fine-tuning on generated COCO images executes successfully for 50,000 steps and produces a fine-tuned f_g model with aligned generated-real representations.
Image captioning fine-tuning with ClipCap + GMAIL on COCO executes successfully without errors using the aligned generated image embeddings.
Image captioning experiments with LLaVA + GMAIL and Llama3 + GMAIL execute successfully on COCO, producing captions evaluated with standard metrics.
Zero-shot image retrieval experiments with CLIP + GMAIL and Long-CLIP + GMAIL execute successfully on COCO and Flickr30k without errors.
Zero-shot image classification experiments with CLIP + GMAIL execute successfully across all eight benchmarks (DTD, Stanford Cars, SUN397, Food 101, Aircraft, Oxford Pets, Caltech 101, ImageNet 1K).
The ablation study on Gen-Real alignment (with vs. without alignment) executes successfully, producing image captioning results on COCO.
The scaling trend experiments with COCO, CC3M, and CC12M generated images execute successfully and produce Flickr30k retrieval results.

The image captioning results for ClipCap + GMAIL match Table 1: B@4=38.12, METEOR=31.67, CIDEr=119.53, SPICE=23.75, ROUGE-L=56.27, WMD=62.16, significantly outperforming the ClipCap baseline (B@4=32.15, CIDEr=108.35).

The image captioning results for Llama3 + GMAIL match Table 1: B@4=50.21, METEOR=38.59, CIDEr=168.53, SPICE=32.58, ROUGE-L=73.29, WMD=80.25, outperforming the Llama3 baseline (B@4=47.36, CIDEr=158.13).
The image captioning results for LLaVA + GMAIL match Table 1: B@4=43.26, METEOR=34.89, CIDEr=146.38, SPICE=27.23, ROUGE-L=65.25, WMD=71.39.
The zero-shot image retrieval results for CLIP + GMAIL on COCO match Table 2: Image-to-Text R@1=56.8, R@5=80.1, R@10=87.2; Text-to-Image R@1=37.5, R@5=62.7, R@10=73.2.
The zero-shot image retrieval results for Long-CLIP + GMAIL on COCO match Table 2: Image-to-Text R@1=62.3, R@5=84.1, R@10=91.2; Text-to-Image R@1=45.6, R@5=69.8, R@10=79.5.

The zero-shot image retrieval results for CLIP + GMAIL on Flickr30k match Table 3: Image-to-Text R@1=47.1, R@5=71.2, R@10=79.6; Text-to-Image R@1=30.2 (note: paper Table 3 shows 30.2 for CLIP+GMAIL vs 24.7 for CLIP baseline with a large gain in text-to-image).

The zero-shot image classification results for CLIP + GMAIL match Table 4: DTD=65.26, Stanford Cars=81.32, SUN397=75.53, Food 101=95.21, Aircraft=37.85, Oxford Pets=95.23, Caltech 101=95.57, ImageNet=77.68, all outperforming CLIP baseline.
The ablation study results match Table 6: adding Gen-Real alignment improves image captioning over the no-alignment baseline by at least 1.97 B@4 (36.15 → 38.12) and 4.18 CIDEr (115.35 → 119.53).

The scaling trend results match Table 7: performance on Flickr30k zero-shot retrieval increases monotonically from COCO (R@1=47.1) to CC3M (R@1=48.6) to CC12M (R@1=50.9) training data for image-to-text retrieval.
The LoRA rank ablation results match Table 11: LoRA rank 4 achieves best performance (B@4=38.12, CIDEr=119.53) compared to rank 2 (B@4=36.85), rank 6 (B@4=37.96), and full fine-tuning (B@4=37.50).

The dual projection ablation results match Table 9: using dual projection with alignment on synthetic data (B@4=38.12, CIDEr=119.53) approaches performance of training on real data (B@4=38.24, CIDEr=119.78), while synthetic without alignment (B@4=36.15, CIDEr=115.35) is substantially worse.

The long caption retrieval results match Table 5: CLIP + GMAIL achieves Image-to-Text R@1=85.2 and Text-to-Image R@1=86.7 on ShareGPT4V, outperforming CLIP baseline (78.2, 79.6); Long-CLIP + GMAIL achieves 97.2 and 96.1.
